title: Namesake

infobox.value: This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. is a person, place, or thing bearing the name of another. Most commonly, it refers to an individual who is purposely named after another[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1) (e.g. [John F. Kennedy Jr](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Jr "John F. Kennedy Jr") would be the namesake of [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy")). In common [parlance](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/parlance "wikt:parlance"), it may mean vice-versa (i.e. referring to the entity for which the second entity is named); in such a case, however, the proper term would be "[eponym](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym")."[[2]](#cite_note-oxford-2)[[3]](#cite_note-collins-3)[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1)[[4]](#cite_note-rhd-4)
Harper, Douglas. ["Namesake"](http://etymonline.com/index.php?allowed_in_frame=0&search=namesake). *Online Etymology Dictionary*. Retrieved 14 March 2016.

See, *e.g.*, [Nowicke, Joan W.](/wiki/Joan_W._Nowicke "Joan W. Nowicke") (September–October 1974). "Three New Species of Tournefortia (Boraginaceae) from the Andes and Comments on the Manuscripts of E. P. Killip". *Bulletin of the Torrey Botanical Club*. **101** (5): 229–234. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Someone or something named after a person

For other uses, see [Namesake (disambiguation)](/wiki/Namesake_(disambiguation) "Namesake (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [eponym](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym"), the original entity bearing a name.

A **namesake** is a person, place, or thing bearing the name of another. Most commonly, it refers to an individual who is purposely named after another[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1) (e.g. [John F. Kennedy Jr](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Jr "John F. Kennedy Jr") would be the namesake of [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy")). In common [parlance](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/parlance "wikt:parlance"), it may mean vice-versa (i.e. referring to the entity for which the second entity is named); in such a case, however, the proper term would be "[eponym](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym")."[[2]](#cite_note-oxford-2)[[3]](#cite_note-collins-3)[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1)[[4]](#cite_note-rhd-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namesake&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The word is first attested around 1635,[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1) and probably comes from the phrase "for one's name's sake",[[2]](#cite_note-oxford-2)[[5]](#cite_note-ahd-5)[[6]](#cite_note-etymon-6)
which originates in English Bible translations as a rendering of a Hebrew idiom meaning "to protect one's reputation" or possibly "vouched for by one's reputation." Examples are in Psalm 23:3, "He leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for His name's sake" (King James Bible, 1604), or in the metrical version "e'en for His own name's sake" (Rous 1641, Scottish Psalter 1650, see [The Lord's My Shepherd](/wiki/The_Lord%27s_My_Shepherd#Text "The Lord's My Shepherd")).

## Proper usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namesake&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Proper usage")]

When *namesake* refers to something or someone who is named after something or someone else, the second recipient of a name is usually said to be the *namesake* of the first. This usage usually refers to humans named after other humans,[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1)[[4]](#cite_note-rhd-4) but current usage also allows things to be or have namesakes.[[2]](#cite_note-oxford-2)[[3]](#cite_note-collins-3) Sometimes the first recipient can also be called the *namesake*;[[1]](#cite_note-mw-1) however, the correct and unambiguous term would be the *[eponym](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym")*.

## Family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namesake&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Family")]

Naming a child after a relative, friend, or well-known person is a common practice in the English-speaking world. Continued practise of naming a child after the parent or grandparent may result in several relatives (e.g. cousins) being namesakes of each other despite not having been named after each other.

Among [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"), it is customary to name a child after a dead relative, such as the child's grandparent, but never after a living person.[[7]](#cite_note-chabad-7) [Sephardic Jews](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews") traditionally are encouraged to name their children after relatives, living or dead.[[7]](#cite_note-chabad-7) Greek families traditionally name a child after its paternal grandparents and the second child of the same sex is named after its maternal grandparents.

### Suffixes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namesake&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Suffixes")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Namesake "Special:EditPage/Namesake") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(June 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

When a son is named after his father, "Jr."/"II", "III'", or another [name suffix](/wiki/Suffix_(name) "Suffix (name)") may be added to the name of the son (and sometimes "Sr." or a prior number to the father's name), in order to distinguish between individuals, especially if both father and son become famous, as in the case of poet [Oliver Wendell Holmes Sr.](/wiki/Oliver_Wendell_Holmes_Sr. "Oliver Wendell Holmes Sr.") and his son, [Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.](/wiki/Oliver_Wendell_Holmes_Jr. "Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr."), an associate justice of the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court").

Sometimes the "Jr." or "Sr." suffix is applied even when the child's legal name differs from that of the parent. One example is that of the singer Hiram King Williams, known professionally as [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams"), and his son Randall Hank Williams, known professionally as [Hank Williams Jr](/wiki/Hank_Williams_Jr "Hank Williams Jr"). Daughters being named after their mothers using similar suffixes is less common. One example is thoroughbred jockey [Rosemary Homeister Jr.](/wiki/Rosemary_Homeister_Jr. "Rosemary Homeister Jr.") whose mother was also a jockey before turning to train. A more archaic method of distinguishing father from son was to follow the name with "the Elder" or "the Younger", respectively, for example [William Pitt the Elder](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Elder "William Pitt the Elder") and [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"). For an extensive list, see [List of people known as the Elder or the Younger](/wiki/List_of_people_known_as_the_Elder_or_the_Younger "List of people known as the Elder or the Younger").

## Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namesake&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other uses")]

Buildings, such as the [Fisher Building](/wiki/Fisher_Building "Fisher Building"), and companies, like the [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), are often named after their founders or owners. Biological species and celestial bodies are frequently named after their discoverers.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Alternatively, their discoverers may name them in honor of others.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Occasionally, material goods, such as toys or garments, may be named after people closely associated with them in the public mind. The [teddy bear](/wiki/Teddy_bear "Teddy bear"), for example, was named after President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), because of a popular story in which the then-President objected to cruel treatment of a bear by hunters.[[10]](#cite_note-loc_teddy-10)

The [fedora](/wiki/Fedora "Fedora") hat may be considered the "namesake" of a fictional character, Princess Fédora Romanoff, from an 1887 play, *[Fédora](/wiki/F%C3%A9dora "Fédora")*, by Victorien Sardou. In her portrayal of that character, [Sarah Bernhardt](/wiki/Sarah_Bernhardt "Sarah Bernhardt") wore a soft felt hat with a center crease, which became known popularly as a "fedora".[[11]](#cite_note-etymon_fedora-11) The same is true of the [trilby](/wiki/Trilby "Trilby") hat, named after the title character of the play *[Trilby](/wiki/Trilby_(play) "Trilby (play)")*.

Several [United States military](/wiki/United_States_military "United States military") aircraft have served as the namesake of previous aircraft. The [Fairchild Republic A-10 Thunderbolt II](/wiki/Fairchild_Republic_A-10_Thunderbolt_II "Fairchild Republic A-10 Thunderbolt II") attack aircraft was named after the WWII-era [Republic P-47 Thunderbolt](/wiki/Republic_P-47_Thunderbolt "Republic P-47 Thunderbolt") fighter-bomber.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The [McDonnell Douglas F-4 Phantom II](/wiki/McDonnell_Douglas_F-4_Phantom_II "McDonnell Douglas F-4 Phantom II") is the namesake of the [McDonnell FH Phantom](/wiki/McDonnell_FH_Phantom "McDonnell FH Phantom").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Uniquely, the [LTV A-7 Corsair II](/wiki/LTV_A-7_Corsair_II "LTV A-7 Corsair II") serves as the namesake of both the [Vought F4U Corsair](/wiki/Vought_F4U_Corsair "Vought F4U Corsair") and the earlier [Vought O2U Corsair](/wiki/Vought_O2U_Corsair "Vought O2U Corsair"), the former also being the namesake of the latter. The newest fighter of the United States, the [Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II") is the namesake of the [United States Army Air Forces](/wiki/United_States_Army_Air_Forces "United States Army Air Forces") twin-engined [Lockheed P-38 Lightning](/wiki/Lockheed_P-38_Lightning "Lockheed P-38 Lightning").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
